questions.question: Who is the main character in the story and what is her real name?
questions.answer: The main character is an eight-year-old girl named Valliammai, who is called 'Valli' for short. She is a curious and self-respecting girl from a village.

questions.question: What was Valli’s favorite pastime?
questions.answer: Valli’s favorite pastime was standing in the front doorway of her house and watching what was happening in the street outside, as she had no playmates her own age.

questions.question: What was the 'overwhelming desire' that Valli developed?
questions.answer: Valli developed an overwhelming desire to ride on the bus that traveled between her village and the nearest town every hour.

questions.question: How did Valli gather information about the bus journey?
questions.answer: Valli listened carefully to the conversations of her neighbors and people who regularly used the bus, and she asked a few discreet questions to find out details about the fare and time.

questions.question: What were the specific details Valli learned about the bus trip?
questions.answer: She learned that the town was six miles from her village, the fare was thirty paise one way, and the trip took forty-five minutes.

questions.question: How did Valli save money for her bus fare?
questions.answer: Valli thriftily saved every stray coin that came her way, resisting the temptation to buy peppermints, toys, balloons, and even skipping the merry-go-round at the village fair.

questions.question: What was Valli’s plan to slip out of the house unnoticed?
questions.answer: Valli knew her mother napped every day from about one to four. She decided to take the one o'clock bus, which would bring her back by two-forty-five.

questions.question: Why did the conductor call Valli 'Madam'?
questions.answer: The conductor called her 'Madam' jokingly because she behaved in a very self-confident, adult-like manner, refusing his help to board the bus and paying her own fare.

questions.question: What did Valli see when she stood up on her seat?
questions.answer: Valli stood up to see over the canvas blind. She saw the canal, palm trees, mountains, and the blue sky on one side, and a deep ditch and green fields on the other.

questions.question: How did Valli react to the elderly man's concern for her?
questions.answer: Valli was annoyed by his attention. When he told her to sit down for her safety, she haughtily replied that she wasn't a child and had paid her full thirty paise like everyone else.

questions.question: What made Valli clap her hands with glee during the journey?
questions.answer: Valli was delighted to see a young cow running right in the middle of the road, tail high in the air, directly in front of the honking bus.

questions.question: Why didn't Valli get off the bus when it reached the town?
questions.answer: Valli didn't get off because she only wanted to experience the bus ride. She didn't have enough money or the desire to explore the town; her goal was just the journey itself.

questions.question: Why did Valli refuse the conductor's offer of a free cold drink?
questions.answer: Valli refused because of her strong sense of self-respect and caution. She did not want to take something for free from a stranger.

questions.question: What sad sight did Valli see on her way back?
questions.answer: Valli saw the same young cow that had been running so joyfully earlier, now lying dead by the roadside, struck by a fast-moving vehicle.

questions.question: How did the sight of the dead cow change Valli’s mood?
questions.answer: The sight dampened her enthusiasm. She no longer wanted to look out the window as the memory of the dead cow haunted her, turning her previous joy into sadness.

questions.question: Who was Valli’s mother talking to when Valli returned home?
questions.answer: Valli’s mother was talking to one of Valli’s aunts—the one from South Street—who was a real 'chatterbox'.

questions.question: What did Valli’s mother say about the world outside?
questions.answer: Her mother remarked that there are many things happening in the world outside and inside our houses that we cannot possibly know about or understand completely.

questions.question: Why did Valli smile to herself at the end of the story?
questions.answer: Valli smiled because she knew exactly what her mother was talking about, having just experienced a part of that 'outside world' her mother thought was unknown to her.

questions.question: What does the story reveal about Valli’s character?
questions.answer: The story reveals Valli is meticulously organized, highly determined, self-respecting, and possesses a mature sense of wonder mixed with a growing understanding of life and death.

questions.question: How does the author use the bus as a symbol of the world?
questions.answer: The bus serves as a bridge between Valli's narrow village life and the vast, bustling town, symbolizing the transition from childhood innocence to the complexities of the wider world.

questions.question: How does the story depict the loss of innocence?
questions.answer: The loss of innocence is depicted through the contrast of the living cow (joy) and the dead cow (grief), marking Valli's first real encounter with the harsh finality of death.

questions.question: What role does the conductor play in the narrative?
questions.answer: The conductor acts as a friendly, light-hearted guide who recognizes and respects Valli's desire to be treated as an equal, despite her young age.

questions.question: What does 'Madam' signify in the title?
questions.answer: It signifies the adult-like dignity and independence Valli strives for during her journey, and how she wants to be perceived by the world.

questions.question: Why is Valli's aunt described as a 'chatterbox'?
questions.answer: This description provides a comic relief and highlights the contrast between the aunt’s trivial talk and the profound life experience Valli has just quietly returned from.

questions.question: Summarize the main message of 'Madam Rides the Bus'.
questions.answer: The story highlights that the quest for knowledge and experience requires courage and planning. It also illustrates that while the world is full of beauty and excitement, it also holds harsh realities like death that one must eventually confront.
